elements.sent-2411-005: Let $F$ be a graph on $r$ vertices and let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-006: Then an $F$-factor in $G$ is a subgraph of $G$ composed of $n/r$ vertex-disjoint copies of $F$, if $r$ divides $n$.
elements.sent-2411-007: In other words, an $F$-factor yields a partition of the $n$ vertices of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-008: The study of such $F$-factors in the Erdős–Rényi random graph dates back to Erdős himself.
elements.sent-2411-009: Decades later, in 2008, Johansson, Kahn and Vu established the thresholds for the existence of an $F$-factor for strictly 1-balanced~$F$ -- up to the leading constant.
elements.sent-2411-010: The sharp thresholds, meaning the leading constants, were obtained only recently by Riordan and Heckel, but only for complete graphs $F=K_r$ and for so-called nice graphs.
elements.sent-2411-011: Their results rely on sophisticated couplings that utilize the recent, celebrated solution of Shamir's problem by Kahn.
elements.sent-2411-012: We extend the couplings by Riordan and Heckel to any strictly 1-balanced $F$ and thereby obtain the sharp threshold for the existence of an $F$-factor.
elements.sent-2411-013: In particular, we confirm the thirty year old conjecture by Rucínski that this sharp threshold indeed coincides with the sharp threshold for the disappearance of the last vertices which are not contained in a copy of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-016: What is the simplest, local obstruction for a graph $G$, on an even number of vertices, to admit a perfect matching? Well, if an isolated vertex exists, a perfect matching cannot.
elements.sent-2411-017: Back in 1966, Erdős and Rényi \cite{erdos1966existence showed that this simplest, local obstruction is indeed the only relevant obstruction, when it comes to the random graph $G(n,p)$, with vertices $[n]=\{1,\dots,n\}$, and where each edge is included independently with probability $p$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: To be precise, recall that a sequence $p^*=p^*_n$ is a sharp threshold for a graph property $\mc E=\mc E_n$ if for all $\eps>0$ and $p\le(1-\eps)p^*$ the random graph $G(n,p)$ does not have $\mc E$ with high probability (whp), that is, with probability tending to one as $n$ increases, while for all $p\ge(1+\eps)p^*$ the random graph $G(n,p)$ does have $\mc E$ whp.
elements.sent-2411-019: Erdős and Rényi \cite{erdos1966existence observed that the sharp threshold for the existence of perfect matchings coincides with the sharp threshold for the disappearance of isolated vertices.}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Induced Edges, Avoidable Configurations And Clean Cycles
elements.sent-2411-090: When trying to construct a coupling between the random $F$-graph $H=\fgraph(n,\pi)$ and the copies of $F$ in the random graph $G=G(n,p)$, one invariably encounters the following fundamental problem.
elements.sent-2411-091: Let $H'$ be the $F$-graph of $G$, meaning that we include each copy of $F$ in $G$ as an $F$-edge in $H'$.
elements.sent-2411-092: For all $0<\pi<1$, $H$ can clearly attain any $F$-graph.
elements.sent-2411-093: But this is not the case for $H'$, due to the existence of so-called 'induced $F$-edges': Assume that there is an $F$-edge $h$, meaning a copy $h$ of $F$, which is not contained in $H$, but that for each (graph) edge $e$ in the copy $h$ of $F$ there exists an $F$-edge $h_e$ in $H$ that covers $e$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Then $H'$ cannot attain $H$, because if the copies $h_e$ of $F$ appear in $G$, then so does the copy $h$ of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-095: Riordan shows that only two types of (minimal) $F$-graphs can induce $F$-edges: so-called `avoidable configurations' and so-called `clean $F$-cycles'.
elements.sent-2411-096: Roughly speaking, the former are given by connected $F$-graphs which have at least two cycles and the latter consist of exactly one cycle, with `cycles' defined such that two hyperedges that overlap on more than two vertices have more than one cycle.
elements.sent-2411-097: He proceeds to show that avoidable configurations do not exist whp in $H$.

elements.sent-2411-098: This leaves us with clean $F$-cycles, which may exist in $H$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Riordan avoids the discussion of these clean $F$-cycles by restricting to the class of graphs $F$ from Theorem \ref{thm:threshold_nice , which he called `nice': A graph with these properties cannot be induced by a clean $F$-cycle.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Heckel \cite{heckel2020random , on the other hand, tweaks Riordan's coupling to deal with the only type of clean $K_3$-cycle which can induce a triangle $K_3$, namely the one of length $3$.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Since we consider the case of general strictly 1-balanced graphs, we also have to deal with clean $F$-cycles.
elements.sent-2411-102: But as opposed to Heckel, we have to consider all types of clean $F$-cycles.
elements.sent-2411-103: While this is a major complication in its own right, there is one particular type of clean $F$-cycle which is particularly demanding: so-called `sparse clean $F$-cycles', which are given by two $F$-edges, meaning two copies of $F$, that share exactly one (graph) edge (and no other vertices).
elements.sent-2411-105: So a clean $F$-cycle $C$ of length $k$ and its shadow graph $C'$, which is obtained by taking the graph union of the $F$-edges in $C$, have the same inclusion probability $\pi^k\approx p^{ke(F)}$, in $H$ and $G$ respectively --- unless $C$ is a sparse clean $F$-cycle, because then we are missing an edge in $C'$.
elements.sent-2411-106: This small specialty of $C$ has significant implications for the entire proof.
elements.sent-2411-107: The treatment of these sparse clean $F$-cycles is one of our two main contributions.
elements.sent-2411-108: Heckel heavily relies on the fact that the expected number of clean $3$-cycles in $H$ (the only relevant type of clean $K_3$-cycle in the setting of \cite{heckel2020random ) matches the expected number of their shadow graphs in $G$ almost exactly.}

elements.sent-2411-111: We do the next best thing: We randomly select a subset of the shadow graphs of sparse clean $F$-cycles in $G$, where we include each such shadow graph independently with probability $p$.
elements.sent-2411-112: This ensures that the number of selected shadow graphs matches the number of sparse clean $F$-cycles in $H$.
elements.sent-2411-113: For our proof, it is extremely useful to model the inclusion in this random subset as follows.

elements.sent-2411-115: We further consider the random graph $G^*=G^*(n,p)$, where we do not only include each potential edge, but also each potential dummy edge independently with probability $p$.
